Transcrição Completa: Get Richer Principles with Jeremy Haynes
[00:00]
"It was all wise, you know, like why to get richer? Like as an example, if my dogs die, I'm going to clone them, you know, if you have some kind of medical issue, you know, you can afford the best of the best."
[00:15]
"As an example, you get cancer, you know, chemotherapy and how that all works. Well, when you're rich and you get cancer, they take your cancer cells. They have like 180 different types of chemotherapy and they test each one against those cancerous cells to see which one's actually most probable to kill your cancer and not kill you in the process."
[00:35]
"An example, what rich people can afford that a regular person or your regular insurance isn't going to pay for you. Okay, all kinds of benefits."
[00:45]
"But I realized once again, all my get-rich stuff was about the whys. And recently at the Q3 mastermind, we had my high performance coach Mark Dama talk and he mentioned something to you guys about my money mindset."
[01:05]
"And you know, I got to keep it real. Sometimes I view talking about these things as kind of cheesy to a degree. I think that all of you already think this stuff."
[01:15]
"Looking at my life and what I've done to transition from, you know, where I started to where I am today. There are some very critical just foundational ways that you have to think in order to be able to grow and maintain constantly getting richer."
[01:35]
"I'm sure you all have little things that you think or little habits that you've developed for yourself that help prop up your ability to go out there and generate revenue with a positive mental frame every day, not get burnt out."
[01:50]
"Not feel like you're working your ass off for like 12 hours a day, and to once again truly maintain being able to do it."
[02:00]
"So anyway, I'm going to go over what from my view are the necessary foundational things you have to think to be able to constantly be on that path and have fun doing it. Are you guys ready to hear those?"
[02:15]
"Let me hear you again. Are you ready to hear them?"
[02:18]
"Okay, great. So, number one, brutal honesty about how you are, why you do the things you do, and what truly drives you to do those things."
[02:30]
"Raise of hands real quick. How many of you would consider me flashy? A couple here and there, right?"
[02:38]
"Well, guys, I'm wearing a f*cking sapphire and diamond Rolex, okay? As an example of a little bit of flash. I have two Ferraris that I park in front of my house so every time I walk past my front door, I can look at them."
[02:55]
"I go pick up a smoothie in a 458. Okay? I love money, okay? I love visuals of money. I love material things. I love feeling rich."
[03:10]
"I like putting on a t-shirt that costs 500 bucks instead of putting on a regular t-shirt that probably feels the exact same. I like sitting courtside at basketball games so a basketball can hit me or a player can get thrown on me."
[03:28]
"I like sitting down in a restaurant and them giving me the bill. I don't even look, you know? I like ordering a dozen things and eating a bite of them and not even taking them home."
[03:40]
"I like first class. I like flying private. Okay, first class feels like a poverty level to me now. Unless it's international. I'm still very poor to fly private internationally."
[03:55]
"Listen, I love materialistic sh*t okay? I don't virtue signal about it. Virtue signaling is one of the things that I believe holds people back from truly getting rich."
[04:08]
"Virtue signaling is essentially lying to yourself, lying to others mainly about your intentions for why you want to make money and why you want to get richer in an effort to be a particular person that you think other people want you to be."
[04:25]
"To get richer, you got to be your truest self. You got to be brutally honest and tapped in to the real motives for why you do what you do."
[04:38]
"I think it was three, four years ago, I got my first migraine with aura symptoms. Okay? Sh*t was insane to me that this even - I thought I had a stroke when I had my first one."
[04:52]
"I had these - it's like when you look at the sun and then look away. You know how you get that spot? It's like I just was reading an article and all of a sudden the words started to swirl on the screen."
[05:08]
"I started to get like that sunspot and it just kept growing. My arm started to go numb from like my pinky up to my elbow. I had no idea what was going on."
[05:20]
"Listen to this. If I get one of those nowadays, do you know what I do? I spend like five grand in a day. I get my doctor to come over to my house. He injects me in the ass with some random sh*t I don't know what it is. Makes me feel better in like minutes."
[05:38]
"Get my chiropractor to come over, snap me up, puts me in a parasympathetic state. I do all these things that cost money to be able to recover from these things and to prevent them in the first place."
[05:52]
"I had my first one again in like two years on Tuesday, as a matter of fact, from a very hardcore workout that Vic and I did outside. Moral of the story is I did the same thing on Tuesday."
[06:05]
"Here's my point in terms of brutal honesty. There's many things that I do to make money in order to avoid having migraines. There's many things that I do to make money in the instance that I have some type of negative health effect."
[06:22]
"Where one day it is a stroke or one day it is some kind of actual thing that's going to prevent me from making money any further. You know how much wealth I accumulate? You know how much I invest?"
[06:35]
"Do you know how much I consider stashing away and putting towards assets solely because of this random health event that I had 3-4 years ago that occasionally occurs once every few years."
[06:48]
"You know, it's a huge drive. It's brutal honesty. It's brutal honesty with myself that helps me as a driver to get richer."
[07:00]
"So, the materialistic stuff, I love it, right? Love it. I love going to restaurants at the best of the best on the planet Earth, eating the best foods."
[07:12]
"I hate going to regular events. Like, I went to a Drake concert the other day. I was in a suite. I hated it, you know? I didn't like it at all."
[07:22]
"You know what I think of myself now? I think I deserve to pay Drake to be somewhere that's more intimate, like hanging out as bros rather than going to a concert. You know, I feel above that level."
[07:38]
"Now, the thing I'm trying to articulate to you here, these are brutal truths that are inside of my brain that I don't ignore, that I don't allow to just sit dormant. Okay?"
[07:50]
"I actively use them as drivers to push me to a point where I'm constantly thinking about them, constantly using them as a motivating factor for me to go out there and make an excess of money above and beyond what I need to just be comfortable and cover my means."
[08:08]
"How often do you hear people say, 'Oh, you just need enough money for like six months of your expenses, you know, you need to invest 10% of your income, and it's going to take you about 40 years, and eventually you'll have enough to not be able to work anymore.'"
[08:25]
"That's so belittling to me to hear that and to act like that's how it is. That's not how it is. You should be making in excess of hundreds of thousands of dollars per month of what you need that you can actively invest and just spend freely on whatever you want."
[08:45]
"You should be able to have an unrealistic age that you choose to retire that you genuinely believe you could do. So, I've asked some of you over the years, when do you want the option to no longer work?"
[09:00]
"Some of you have said some audacious sh*t like you're like 30 years old. You said you want to stop working at like 35. That's doable if you actually believe that it's possible and you go out there and you work your ass off for 5 years making an excess of cash."
[09:18]
"But if you don't have people around you or if you yourself aren't hyper obsessed with these unrealistic goals that normal people would tell you are unrealistic, guess what happens? You yourself become average, mundane, slow."
[09:35]
"You can't consider them excess is my point. Brutal honesty for me is that these things are the norm for me, not excess, not things that I want to have cut out optionally if I contract, things that must be done."
[09:52]
"Give you an example. If I go to a basketball game, I'm not going if I'm not sitting on the court. It's not happening. Okay, that's a standard. It's a choice, but it's a standard."
[10:08]
"I own like six cars. I will not drive downtown 45 minutes or an hour to go to that game. You know what I will do? I will get the Sprinter van that is like a mobile luxury living room and be driven there instead."
[10:25]
"Brutal honesty about the standards that you want to live at and not capitulating beneath them for any reason. It's in a sense a punishment to have a standard and to not be able to meet it that then acts as a driver to be able to get richer."
[10:45]
"I find so many people just lie to themselves along the way about the things that they truly want and the things that truly drive them."
[10:55]
"I'll give you a great exercise as an example of something you could put into practice that you can really see whether you're honest or not. If something happened to you right now, some kind of health crisis that disabled your ability to work, how would you leave off your wife? How would you leave off your kids?"
[11:15]
"The exercise is very simple. You write down a plan that you have to hand off to that person if and when something happens to you. Like as an example, here's my bank account information. Here's the asset account information."
[11:32]
"I had like a three-page plan that really exposed to me my current financial conditions, okay? And looking at it made me brutally honest about whether I was currently satisfied with what would happen."
[11:48]
"This exercise puts you in a mental frame of extreme motivation while you're capable of working to be able to go out there and put your loved ones in a position of prosperity rather than in a f*cked up situation."
[12:05]
"There's a great book called You Need More Money by one of my mentors, guy named Matt Monero. And in the book, it's a very simple lesson. He had, I believe it was his uncle or his brother or somebody like that. Regular working man. Guy had kids and a wife. No life insurance policy, no assets, paycheck to paycheck. The guy dies."
[12:28]
"Do you know how f*cked up of a situation that is to leave your kids and your wife in? Not only did they have to deal with a death, but immediately have to deal with extreme financial insecurity right after."
[12:42]
"This is a brutal honesty exercise to be able to essentially expose financial vulnerability in what you currently do and to then leverage it as a motivating factor."
[12:55]
"Number two, being the rich person that you think a rich person is. Really think about this one. For all of you in here, you have to make a certain amount to even be in this room."
[13:10]
"Each one of you to a degree are somewhat of the rich person that you've always thought a rich person is - the habits they have, the media they consume."
[13:22]
"I got rich enough I found myself reading things like The Economist. Never liked The Economist. Why did I all of a sudden reach a certain financial level and then start reading that? Because I had that characteristic in my head that that's what a rich person does at that specific level of richness that I achieved."
[13:42]
"Why do you go to Equinox instead of a normal gym? Why do you eat the foods that you eat instead of eating ramen anymore?"
[13:52]
"I pulled up to a McDonald's the other day in one of my Ferraris and I was like, this is cool. I thought to myself, I've seen a picture of this, I think."
[14:05]
"Being the rich person that you think a rich person is. You'll play it out subconsciously whether you're aware of it or not. So point number two about getting richer, you got to bring it from the subconscious to the conscious."
[14:20]
"You got to proactively think. What is a rich person to you? And I don't mean just a random rich person. I mean, what do you actually view based on, let's say, a 100k a month or a million a month or 5 million a month. What does that person do?"
[14:38]
"And surprisingly, when you list that off, those are the things that you'll eventually do when you reach that level, and you'll kind of unknowingly grow into that character at that said level."
[14:52]
"The good news is you can have some awesome characteristics in your head about what you think a rich person is and you'll grow into an awesome person. The bad news is you might have some terrible characteristics in your head about what you view a rich person as and you might unfortunately grow into some very negative characteristics."
[15:10]
"Give you an example. If you think rich people are assholes, guess what you'll become? You'll be an asshole. If you think that rich people are super direct, you know, and that to you means potentially being an asshole to people, you're going to be an asshole."
[15:28]
"If you go out there and you think that rich people hoard all their money and never spend it, it's like you're going to be a frugal individual as a rich person once you finally get there."
[15:40]
"All the movies you've watched, okay? All the little clips you've seen on social media, all the books you've read, all the random things that you've heard about how rich people are, those are all the characteristics that form into an identity that you grow into when you eventually reach whatever you view as a rich person."
[16:00]
"The good news is we can proactively build the character just by being self-aware that that's how it all works. This is a self-awareness exercise that you need to do when it comes to point number two."
[16:15]
"You have to be able to break down what the identity, what the characteristics of that individual look like. And you have to really flesh out. Sometimes you're not going to be able to pull them all out in real time."
[16:28]
"Sometimes you got to kind of look at yourself and just ask and analyze. Do I have some type of negative characteristic that is really just something that I think a rich person should have? And now I'm a rich person. So now I have that characteristic."
[16:45]
"You are fluid as a character. Your identity, all the characteristics of yourself, every personality trait you have, how you speak, the accent you talk with, all of it, every single thing is a choice."
[17:00]
"You ever met somebody who didn't have an accent? They move somewhere, then you talk to them a couple years later and all of a sudden they have a thick accent for that region. How the f*ck did that work? It's assimilation."
[17:15]
"When you reach a certain level, you're going to assimilate to what you think that level is. If you're not going to practice the self-awareness and build the character, you're just going to naturally become whatever character it is that you think that you should be for that level."
[17:35]
"Number three, efficiency. You know, several years ago, I had a video called the compound life. I live it right now. The compound life is very simple. Why go to the doctor when the doctor can come to you?"
[17:50]
"Why go to the chiropractor when the chiropractor could come to you? Why go to the barber shop when the barber can come to you? Why go to the boxing gym when the boxing guy can come to you?"
[18:05]
"Why would you ever go to a grocery store when they can deliver you groceries? I just had my Range Rover the other day. Marina rolled down the window and the motor just f*cked up. The window just dropped."
[18:20]
"Do you think I'm going to drive that Range Rover to the dealership and sit there while it gets serviced? Or like take an Uber back? It's like, no, it's a tow truck. The tow truck picks up the car and takes it over there."
[18:35]
"I haven't seen a dealership in years. I have bought every car I own without even going to a dealership. They send me videos, you know, they walk me through it virtually."
[18:48]
"My point is, efficiency means you buy back time. However, you don't want to optimize fun out of the equation when it comes to efficiency."
[19:00]
"What you're going to have occur is you're going to essentially bring everything into one place. Like here, if I want to have fun, I got a whole disc golf course in my front yard. I got ATVs. I can just go rip them around on the farms around here."
[19:18]
"I can do donuts in one of the cars. I can go on my pool in the backyard. It's like a hotel pool. I can play tennis on the property, basketball on the property. I can ride horses in the back of the property."
[19:35]
"I can do damn near every single thing I would ever want to do from an extracurricular perspective on the property. I got a gym on the property. Every single thing happens here."
[19:48]
"That allows me to have essentially unlimited time freedom because all those things take a significant amount of drive time, wait time, and then actually doing those things somewhere else."
[20:02]
"You end up wasting literally weeks out of your year just in drive time alone, let alone all the excess wait time. Like you go sit and wait at a doctor, dude, I can do whatever I want. My doctor literally just walks into my house and just immediately starts servicing me."
[20:20]
"Those little minutes add up tremendously over the course of an entire year, over the course of an entire decade. A non-exaggerated statistic is I will literally save about a year and a half worth of just drive time not having to go and doing those things and doing them here instead."
[20:38]
"When I say don't optimize fun out of the equation, there are things that you're going to want to go do that obviously you're not going to be able to do at your house. You don't have to over optimize every single thing."
[20:52]
"You still want to go out and do things that you enjoy. It might be going to restaurants, you know, going to games, going to hang out with friends, whatever. Be kind of careful with this one when it comes specifically to like fun categories."
[21:08]
"Number four, it's very important to understand this. There's short-term and long-term decisions that impact you temporarily and permanently. As an example of that, debt."
[21:22]
"There's things that you can do that truly will not affect you negatively at all that you'll be able to overcome in like a day if you want to undo it. There's other things that you're going to be locked into for like years if you go about locking yourself down into them."
[21:38]
"Give you an example. Let's say you buy a house. In that housing market currently, there's a high probability that if there's a little more inventory that hits the market here pretty soon, it's going to be a pretty extreme crash."
[21:52]
"You're going to have what's called negative equity. Meaning, you're going to, let's say, own a house for a million bucks. Market crashes $300,000. Your house is now worth 700 grand. You put down 200 grand on that house. That means you finance 800 grand."
[22:10]
"You're in a position now where your home's worth 700. If you wanted to sell it, you'd have to dish out an extra 100K just to get out of that home in the instance that you wanted to undo that decision."
[22:25]
"You might not always have 100K laying around to be able to get out of that house. And that would be an example of potentially financially burdening yourself with something that becomes somewhat permanent to a degree."
[22:40]
"If I just ignore my health, okay, constantly and I'm never doing things like working out, never recovering, I'm never actively doing things to improve my physical circumstances, eventually you kind of get permanently locked into a body that's going to just weigh you down."
[22:58]
"Getting richer for me as a summarization of this specific characteristic is not being tied down. I love the fluidity of situations that are easy to undo if I don't like them or if I don't want to be in them anymore."
[23:15]
"People come to me all the time, agency deals, and they're like, 'Dude, should I lock this guy into a contract for like a year or two?' I do all my deals month-to-month."
[23:28]
"I have clients, one guy in particular, I won't say his name out of respect. He's one of the best people ever to work with, you know, an amazing mentor. Guy's doing, you know, eight figures plus a year. Great guy to work with."
[23:42]
"All of a sudden, when the elections occurred with the Trump and Biden thing, he becomes the most right-wing hardcore political person I've ever met in my entire life. It was impossible to have a conversation with the guy without him bringing up some type of hyper-political situation that I don't give a sh*t about."
[24:00]
"I don't want to be in that deal, you know? I want to get out of the deal. If I was in a contract with the guy, I'd be stuck in the deal. I want to be able to undo situations like that when they occur."
[24:15]
"I like the opportunity if I'm in a lease and I don't like the home. Let's say this house gets riddled with mold. Okay, I pay two months worth of rent. I'm out. I'm gone, rather than having to go through the process of selling it."
[24:32]
"You're in a marriage. Do you have a prenup? You know, God forbid you want to undo that. Are you in a situation where you can reverse course?"
[24:42]
"Now, I want to be clear. It's also important to be able to fully commit to things in life. And it's okay to take on long-term things that technically tie you down. You just have to make sure that you're considering whether you're getting tied down long-term or short-term in every decision you make."
[25:00]
"For me, being a rich person is being smart, you know, and not getting caught in those foolish situations."
[25:10]
"I won't go too into this one. It's very self-explanatory. Debt can be an amazing tool. You know, as an example, you want to go out and buy a sh*tload of real estate. Be pretty dumb to buy it all cash."
[25:25]
"It's a great opportunity to leverage your money to be able to purchase some real estate just by putting some cash down and going into debt. However, you got to make sure obviously you're cash flowing positively on that type of debt because then the debt is covered by the asset that you acquired."
[25:42]
"There's other types of debt that you're going to acquire like a loan is an example. Maybe a high interest loan at that that has a three to five year duration of paying it down."
[25:55]
"People who use Stripe. You have options like Stripe Capital, okay, where they'll give you the ability to take essentially a wage garnish against every dollar you process through their payment processor to make repayments on the loans that you've taken out."
[26:12]
"But there's other debts that are going to eat you alive and they're going to totally take away freedom from you and force you to essentially work in your business as a debt slave."
[26:25]
"It's going to remove all the fun out of it. It's going to remove every ounce of this is just a leisurely thing I enjoy and I get to spend my money freely on things I actually want all the way down to I got to make this payment this month otherwise I'm going to have to liquidate some assets."
[26:42]
"To be clear, debt can be great, but don't let debt f*ck you."
[26:48]
"Number six. I love this one. Back when I worked with Grant about a decade ago, Grant taught me this one, and it's amazing. He taught this to me by having me go and spend my entire paycheck."
[27:05]
"He was like, 'What are you going to do with your check today, Jeremy?' And I was like, 'I don't know, you know, I don't have any bills I got to pay right now. I'm probably just going to save it.'"
[27:18]
"And he was like, 'That's pretty dumb. You should go out there and spend it. What do you want to buy right now?' And I was like, 'Well, you know, I could really use some new clothes. You know, I kind of feel a little below the means I should in your office here. Everybody dresses really nice.'"
[27:35]
"And he's like, 'Great. How much did you make?' And I was like, 'About three grand.' He's like, 'Go spend literally all of it. And if you don't, I'm going to fire you.'"
[27:48]
"I told him I spent it all, you know, but I didn't spend all of it. I spent most of it and I came back and I told him I did. So we'll cut this part of the video just in case."
[28:00]
"But moral of the story is what it did is it taught me a very important lesson. What it did is it empowered me to go out and believe that I could earn that money back rather than having to constantly save to be able to purchase different things."
[28:18]
"It flipped it from I need to acquire money and hoard it to okay, I want that thing. How much does it cost? And who's going to give me the money to be able to go buy that thing? It flipped me from a saver to an earner."
[28:35]
"Now, I just want to disclose here. I put it in parenthesis here. Rich people technically still save money, but we do it through an act of investing rather than through technical saving. We don't just put money into an account."
[28:52]
"I want to also put a caveat on this. Nowadays with interest rates where they are, I mean, it's okay to put money into an account and let it grow 5% or whatever interest rates are going to be at a year from now, that's awesome because it's technically making you money."
[29:08]
"Saving money in a traditional account where you're getting like 0.1 or whatever it is nowadays, that's sh*t. That's what poor people do. Rich people, once again, they save through investments."
[29:22]
"But the main mindset here in point number six is just the idea that we earn our way to our things that we want in life. We do not attempt to save our way to those things. We attempt to essentially throw lump sums at things by earning that lump sum in the act of acquiring that thing."
[29:42]
"This mindset will turn you into a monster because of the underlying belief system behind it. The underlying belief system is I believe I can go out there and earn that money to acquire that said thing rather than having to be a wage slave and saving our way to the point where we can acquire things."
[30:00]
"And I mean this for the most audacious sh*t okay? If you want to go out there and acquire like a $5 million property and it's going to cost you a million dollars down, I mean it like how - instead of thinking, okay, I could chunk my way there with $10-$100,000 lump sums."
[30:20]
"When you just ask yourself, how could I actually acquire that million dollar just at one time? It unlocks a level of thinking that most people don't get the opportunity to leverage to be able to go out there and deploy as motivation to acquire that said thing all at once."
[30:38]
"So when you save, you technically think small. When you earn and you just consider how could I get that lump sum at once, it immediately transitions to big think and you accomplish things faster and you move through cycles quicker and you advance faster."
[30:55]
"Number seven, there will be some highs and lows. There'll be positions in your financial life where you'll just be absolutely flush and there'll be times where you're somewhat contracted and you got to be in a position where both your income and your expenses are fluid and they move together."
[31:15]
"One way that rich people really get f*cked is they inflate their expenses to a point along with their income rising and then when their income slightly contracts, they have a really hard time cutting those expenses."
[31:30]
"It's a great book. It's called Evolutionary Psychology. It's one of my favorite books and I honestly probably one of my most referenced books. In it, it has a very simple lesson. Men, especially when we climb the economic and social ladder of life, have a really hard time with moving back down the ladder when necessary."
[31:52]
"It's one of the hardest things to give up is the status or the position that you have managed to climb to in both your eyes and others eyes. But there will be instances in your life where you do need to have a slight contraction to not go into financial ruin."
[32:10]
"You have to be able to be fluid with your expenses specifically and you have to be very aware once again. You are going to want to cling on for dear life to whatever level you've climbed up highest to and it'll be really hard for you to essentially admit to self and to others that you're just not at that level right now."
[32:30]
"Doesn't mean you won't ever be there again, but it does mean you have to essentially give some things up that way you don't go into financial ruin."
[32:40]
"I put a great photo to pair with this one. It's a story of a monkey that found a nut in a tree stump. Monkey puts its hand in, grabs the nut, and when it's trying to pull its hand out, it can't because when it made the fist, that's unfortunately too big to get out of the hole in the stump where the nut was."
[33:00]
"So, here's the monkey trying to get out of the stump. And unfortunately, here comes a predator. And the predator is going to eat this monkey alive. What's the monkey got to do to get out of this situation?"
[33:15]
"Let go of the nut. It's that simple, right? So many great men die financially because they can't let go of the nut. It's that simple."
[33:28]
"What's the nut sometimes is what you got to ask yourself that you got to let go of so you don't die. So your situation doesn't contract to a point that's hard to recover from."
[33:42]
"Just kind of be aware of this one. I don't think many of you or any of you are necessarily in this situation right now. It's a somewhat hopeful rarity for you to be in this kind of position. Remember it regardless."
[33:58]
"Number eight, you know, one of the most interesting things that happens when I get an inner circle student that gets to 100K a month, you know what occurs? I don't hear from them."
[34:12]
"Like some of y'all come into the group, you're at like 20 grand a month, you get to 100K a month, and then you just go ghost. There's people in this very room right now who have done this."
[34:25]
"There's people in the group that you probably as a new member don't even know of who have been in here for years. I haven't seen them for years, but they're still a part of the group. I'll talk to them like, I don't know, once a year."
[34:38]
"It's like, dude, you're doing 1.2 mill a year. What kind of mindset do you have to be too good to continue the relationship with somebody who helped propel you to a point where you got to your highest financial income yet?"
[34:55]
"But yet most commonly the people who get to that 100k a month number are the people who go ghost. Whereas believe it or not like I'll use Chris as an example. Chris comes into the group doing a million a month already."
[35:10]
"Dude, Chris shows up to the first mastermind. Chris gets on one-on-one calls with me. Chris makes - how much did you make in the first like week of being in the group? How much more?"
[35:22]
"More than a million a year on average."
[35:28]
"So he added an extra essentially let's call it 80-83k a month in like a week of being a part of the group. He still sits here with us right now. He's now going to speak in front of us today and give us some lessons."
[35:42]
"It's like Chris is routinely tapped in. I can help guys like Chris make an exceptional - I can help get Chris no joke to 5 million a month I'm sure. And you know what I bet? I bet the entire time he'll be tapped in."
[36:00]
"I bet there won't be one instance throughout that period of time unless he goes into a hyper-focused state where he's going to be tapped out, not talking to us, not being active."
[36:12]
"And here's my point, okay? The people who are most tapped in are the people typically above about 300K a month and below about 80ish-K a month."
[36:25]
"For some reason that 81k to like 299K those surprisingly are some of the most untapped people. They're the people who finally think - they typically what happens in my opinion they reach a level financially that they think is a lot of money."
[36:42]
"Their lifestyle change drastically. They get to spend more than they've ever done before. Things are really changing for them in a lot of ways. And because of that they view themselves in a sense as like too good for help anymore."
[36:58]
"They kind of think in their eyes, you know, I've made it. I've got it figured out. I don't need help anymore."
[37:08]
"Dude, no joke. The richest people I know, and I mean like guys, like people have sold companies for hundreds of millions of dollars. I actively get to talk with, dude, they are tapped in to things that I'm teaching them and helping them."
[37:25]
"They're constantly asking for help. They're constantly on the hunt for that next nugget that helps them make even more than they already have."
[37:35]
"One of the biggest booby traps when you're getting richer is this little comfort zone where you think you're too good for the help. And I'm not saying this as a bias to be the one who helps you."
[37:48]
"I'm saying it as a general - you become like you stop reading books, you stop asking for help from your friends, you stop being transparent with people."
[38:00]
"Very important thing that I've learned throughout my career of getting richer is the best possible way to continue getting richer is to just continue to ask for help and to continue to be open and to continue to educate."
[38:15]
"Such a simple thing - I don't want to make it sound cheesy but like don't stop reading books. Don't stop taking courses. Don't stop literally paying people for help. Don't stop asking your friends for help."
[38:30]
"That is one of the best possible things that you could do that I've learned through the years that moves people forward most. Have you seen it in yourself throughout your lifetime at this point? Have you seen it in others that you know of? It's detrimental booby trap."
[38:48]
"Okay, number nine. This one's one of my favorites. Raise your hand if you know what an echo chamber is. Raise your hand if you do not know what an echo chamber is."
[39:00]
"Okay, so I'm going to give you an example. Let's say that I love Donald Trump, okay? And I just can't get enough of the guy. I think he's president right now. You know, I'm that kind of crazy person, okay?"
[39:15]
"And what kind of media do you think I consume to reinforce that worldview constantly to me? I'm like on Q Anon forums, you know? I'm talking - I'm in Facebook groups. I'm looking at content about it. I'm watching content about it."
[39:35]
"Think about that type of person real quick and think about what type of media they likely consume to constantly reinforce that world view is true. Now do that for what a rich person would need to do to constantly reinforce getting richer."
[39:52]
"What type of media would you have to constantly flow through your senses? And I mean this on everything. Like if I get on Instagram, go look at my Instagram following. I just look at massive yachts, multi-hundred million dollar cribs, you know, jewelry, expensive cars."
[40:10]
"If I open up my Instagram explore feed, I guarantee it's all Ferraris. Literally. Look, tell me I'm wrong, Vic. Name one poor person thing in that entire Instagram explore thing. Nothing. Nothing."
[40:28]
"Every piece of media I consume. Okay, I actively condition my algorithms to feed me rich person echo chamber content."
[40:40]
"I want - here's what that does. That car - those - I don't think my cars are jack sh*t. I think my cars are very under the level that I should be at. Okay, but a lot of people are like, 'Oh, this is super nice.' They're not. They're regular cars."
[40:58]
"They're regular cars. Sure, they're a couple hundred grand. Who cares? They're cheap. You know why I think that? And I genuinely believe it? Because I'm looking at like $4 million Bugattis, a million dollar Ferrari Daytonas, a million dollar Lamborghini SVJs, and it conditions the worldview those things are cheap."
[41:20]
"I pay 19K a month in rent. I am excited, enthused to pay 100k a month in rent. I look forward to the day when life opens up to that. I view that as cheap."
[41:35]
"I view what I pay right now as literally cheap and a good deal because I'm looking at people like Messi who are paying 300k a month in rent, 500k a month in rent constantly."
[41:48]
"I follow these pages on Instagram, traded Miami, traded New York, traded LA, just constantly promoting multi-figure, nine figure real estate that people are out there buying."
[42:02]
"I'm constantly thinking the amount I make is not sh*t. Why? Because like what I shared with you guys just like two days ago, Loom, the video software, got bought for 975 million. Are we making any money right now? You know, no. No, we're not."
[42:22]
"I have four to five of those types of acquisitions I put in front of myself every day just looking at people raising a fckload of money, making a fckload of money, you know, just crushing it."
[42:35]
"And then what's it do? It makes how I am now seem very below what I'm capable of. All media consumption has to condition you. And that's one example of it."
[42:50]
"These could also be for belief systems and just world views overall. Okay, I have not consumed a piece of political content like ever. I like as soon as I see it, not interested. Not interested. Not interested. Not interested."
[43:08]
"I'm not saying those things don't matter. I'm not saying those things don't exist. I'm saying I don't give a sh*t about them. You know why? Because it doesn't reinforce my worldview that I want to see."
[43:22]
"You know, I don't want to be caught up that World War III could potentially occur. What can I do about that? You know, how does that help me get richer? How does that fear, you know?"
[43:35]
"I don't - you know how many crazy ass conspiracy theories there are out there that are probably true? I don't consume any of them because none of them benefit me and help me get richer."
[43:48]
"You know how much like Dave Ramsey content's out there or like - oh man, there's so much of that content. I will not consume any of it because none of it benefits me and the worldview that I want to maintain."
[44:05]
"The media that you consume, okay? I dare you to talk to me about something I don't care about. Some of you in this room may have even experienced me doing this to you where I will literally look you in the eyes and be like, 'I don't give a sh*t what you're talking about right now. Stop talking to me about it.'"
[44:25]
"You know, and I will be direct and honest with you about that because I genuinely don't give a sh*t about all the things that don't reinforce the worldview that I want to see and I want to experience."
[44:38]
"I want to thrive in that self-built and self-reinforced echo chamber."
[44:45]
"Number 10. The healthy man has a thousand wishes. The sick man has one. Pretty self-explanatory. Days I have migraines, I don't give a sh*t about money. I don't care about money. I care about not having a migraine."
[45:02]
"You got to remember, money aside, you better prioritize your health. Better exercise routinely. You better actively invest into recovery."

"You avoid those kind of thoughts by essentially actively investing into your health, recovery, and mitigation of those things occurring before you actually get into those conditions."

"Number 11, your partner and those closest to you. And this could be anybody. This could be your staff. This could be people obviously that you're in active relationships with. This could be your friends. This could be your family."
[46:08]
"You have to have people around you that are actively reinforcing how your character is in your eyes. I'm going to tell you a story real quick. I don't know if I should say this girl's name. I'm going to say a fake name instead. Let's call her Samantha."
[46:25]
"Okay, so you know this is back when I was dating. First of all, there's a great quote. Who knows the perfect apple better? The man who's eaten a thousand apples or the man who's had three apples."
[46:40]

[46:58]

[48:00]

[49:02]

[49:15]

[49:30]

[49:42]
"So, we get back to the penthouse. [50:02]

[50:20]

[50:52]

[51:08]

[51:42]
"And dude, wash my hands of that one after that. Jesus Christ, that was a stressful two weeks."
[51:50]
"Okay, this is an example what I'm talking about. Although hopefully I pray for you, you never have to deal with a girl like Samantha. Some of you might and some of you unfortunately will just kind of like fall into a situation of tolerance."
[52:08]

[52:22]

[52:32]

[52:48]
"Almost done. Number 12. You have to be fully bought in and 100% committed. If you go into anything not fully bought in on doing it, you're gonna get mediocre results, you're gonna get mundane, just everything's going to be average."
[53:08]
"From relationships to making money to education, you have to be 1,000% sold on whatever it is you are going to commit to and that you are going to do within your lifetime."
[53:22]
"To a regular person, you have to come off like borderline delusional there. How many people do you think are out there that genuinely don't believe we'll ever touch Mars, let alone live on it?"
[53:38]
"Then you got guys like Elon Musk who genuinely believe that not only are we going to live on Mars, that we're going to do it within his current lifetime, okay, who are actively taking actions on a day-to-day basis to do it and make it possible."
[53:55]
"Do you think he gives a sh*t about the people who thinks that that's not going to happen? It's like surprisingly there's a lot of rich people out there who capitulate to other people's views of what they think are possible rather than what they view are possible themselves."
[54:12]
"You have to be the utmost sold on whatever you believe. As an example, when I first told myself, I'm going to make 100k a month per deal off of 10 deals. That's what's going to be my million-dollar month."
[54:28]
"I had nobody, not one person in my entire life that had done that. I was unaware of anybody who had done that. I never heard of anybody with such an audacious goal."
[54:42]
"And anybody that I told it to before I did it, guess what? Do you think they believed that it was possible? I've done it many times and I tell people about it and people still think it's not possible."
[54:58]
"Do you think those people are ever going to do it? Do you think the people who who kind of believe that it's possible are going to do it? No. Only the people who are the ones who are sold that it's doable do it."
[55:12]
"Those who have any doubt, and I mean one percent of doubt, are f*cked and will not make it happen. And it's that simple."
[55:22]
"Without the fully bought-in view. I'm talking the mitigation of all skepticism. That's where the money is. That is like such a principle for me that's gotten me richer throughout my life has just been this - the best word is delusional."
[55:40]
"This like just outlandish view of what I think can be done. But it's realistic. That's what I want to make clear. 100k a month off of one deal. That's very realistic. You know why? Because at the time I came up with that, I was already making people a million a month net. I just needed to get 10% of it."
[56:00]
"It's not like I hadn't got somebody to that level before. I just needed a cut. That's very realistic. It's a little different if you're in a position where once again, you've never seen it done."
[56:15]
"There's somebody that you know in this group that says things like I want to be a trillionaire okay like possible not saying it's not but you know the guy does nothing to become a trillionaire."
[56:30]
"The guy does no actions - like doesn't have the kind of businesses to become a trillionaire. It's like it's kind of what's called ego delusion. There's a difference between what's actually possible and still a big think versus what's you know, borderline just egotistical to say for no reason."
[56:50]
"You know, truly it's possible, but it's like even the guy himself like not very bought in on it being done. No actions that are being taken to do it."
[57:02]
"It's like you once again you got to take action at the level of guys like Elon Musk to become a trillionaire, like Zuckerberg to become a trillionaire. The guy does nothing for that kind of stuff."
[57:15]
"My point is that's what's called ego delusion. You don't want to have ego delusion. You want to have realistic big outlandish just delusional sh*t that you believe to be real with subtle signs that it's possible and get fully bought in that it's doable."
[57:35]
"I just want to be clear. It's a difference once again between ego delusion on what's possible and making sh*t that is realistic and then fully being bought in on it and then accomplishing it kind of outlandish delusional this is possible thoughts."
[57:52]
"You don't want to fall victim to the ego delusion. Do things that are realistic."
[58:00]
"Number 13. So there is a very high probability that along this path of getting richer that you will genuinely believe that it is never enough and that can be very depressing if you allow it to be."
[58:18]
"You have to - this might sound a little cheesy but you have to actively practice gratitude. You know, as I mentioned, I don't view any of my current circumstances as nearly enough. Very, very far from it in a very unsettling, almost uncomfortable way."
[58:35]
"It's just I'm so far from where I want to be. But I'm also very grateful that I have all the things that I've got and I'm also very grateful I made all the progress that I've made."
[58:48]
"But I am very very very unsettled internally with where I'm currently at. And that helps drive me to continue moving forward. But like I said, it can also be very depressing if you don't actively practice gratitude."
[59:05]
"And like I said, you don't want to fall into that kind of depression because that won't help you get richer. That's going to hold you back."
[59:15]
"I want to be clear when I say this. It's a really good principle to have a level of discomfort and dissatisfaction with what you've currently got going on. But in telling you to practice this, it's also a little caveat that it could kind of fck you up mentally if you don't actively practice the I still got good sht going on for me kind of thinking."
[59:38]
"Number 14. So, the best state to be in is flow. And flow, from my perspective, it just naturally occurs when we're just constantly moving between projects and tasks that we actively want to do."
[59:52]
"Whatever we actually give a sht about doing in a given moment, that we actually get to flow from that to the next thing to that we give a sht about to the next thing that we give a sh*t about, that to me is flow."
[1:00:08]
"That's where I get the most done. That's where I can sit there for hours in a row and just knock out light years worth of work in like hours."
[1:00:18]
"However, there will be things that you do not want to do. Like, how many of you like going to the DMV? I never met one human being on earth that likes the DMV. How many of you like going to the dentist?"
[1:00:35]
"Thing is, there's all kinds of things like the dentist and the DMV that are going to pop up that are just day-to-day tasks you've got to accomplish. Yeah, you can hire out doing them, but once again, there's things that you do have to do yourself occasionally that you're not going to want to do."
[1:00:52]
"I want to be clear when I say this. You want to have times in the day that you do those things and then you want to allow a majority of your day to just flow through the things that you actually give a sh*t about doing."
[1:01:08]
"This is one of the best ways to perpetuate never burning out to love what you do to just genuinely sit down and like I love what I'm sitting here doing right now kind of thinking going through your head without having to force yourself into thinking it."
[1:01:25]
"Just simply block out a minority of your day for the things you don't want to do. Knock it out. Do it at whatever time you like. Make sure you get them done and then just let the rest of the day flow between whatever you actually give a sh*t about doing."
[1:01:42]
"Huge win for me when it comes to actively implementing that. Took me a few years to really learn."
[1:01:50]
"Number 15. You got to live in places that fuel you and do not erode away at your potential earnings. I grew up in Ohio in a small city of about 50,000ish people."
[1:02:05]
"Most people there were factory workers or just like average income there I think today is about $42,000. That's an example of an environment that would erode away at my potential because of the thinking that goes on in that type of environment."
[1:02:22]
"There's not a lot of like uplifting inspiring things to me. Versus here in Miami, I am constantly bombarded with people far richer than me."
[1:02:35]
"My environment is constantly reinforcing the way that I view the environment should be. It helps me thrive mentally and it helps me feel a part of something that is much bigger than me."
[1:02:48]
"It makes me feel small, which is great for me because I don't want to be a big fish in a small pond. I want to be a small fish in the ocean trying to get bigger."
[1:03:00]
"That type of thinking, that dynamic mentally is tremendous for what it does for your growth potential in comparison to the opposite, which is once again big fish, small pond."
[1:03:15]
"Your environment is huge. If you get burnt out of wherever you live, just move. It's one of the best ways to change and uplift all the existing productivity habits that you've established that are environmentally triggered."
[1:03:32]
"Move to better cities. You know, just move to places that fuel you. If you notice in any way, shape, or form that your household or the city that you live in are just eating away at you and making you feel like you're slow and kind of holding you back, it's the easiest thing to change."
[1:03:50]
"And it's tremendous what it does for your growth when doing so."
[1:03:55]
"Number 16. This one's one of my favorites. I put the word responsibly as a caveat. You want to spend money freely like it's always going to come back. But you want to do so responsibly."
[1:04:12]
"Once again, not just telling you to go blow your wad and then just expect it to come back. I want you to genuinely believe this money I'm about to spend is so easy for me to earn back. It's already coming back like a boomerang."
[1:04:28]
"This mindset helps condition what we need to be thinking to eventually do it on a greater and greater level."
[1:04:38]
"Let me give you an example. When I first moved to Miami, I moved into the hood on accident. I didn't have enough money to come down here and see where I was moving. So, on a map, it looked like I was moving into downtown. In reality, I moved into the trap."
[1:04:55]
"There's a project complex a block away. Like, this was some fcked up sht. And I was concerned. Like, it was a sad moment when we pulled up to that house."
[1:05:08]
"Anyway, it was 950 bucks a month. I had a roommate at the time, so split that in half, that's what the rent was. And I'm like, you walk to the corner store, there's like 30 dudes outside selling drugs kind of environment."
[1:05:25]
"With like cars with the big spinners with their doors all open playing crazy good music that you never heard of before, you know, hard rap. And moral of the story, you know, it was the first step of coming into Miami."
[1:05:40]
"It took me about 8-9 months to be able to get out of that environment and be able to go to the next place. The next place was $1,650 a month. I didn't have a roommate, though."
[1:05:52]
"So, I went from essentially like, let's call it 475 a month in rent to 1650 a month in rent. That felt doable. You know, I had a job that was paying me 5k a month now."
[1:06:08]
"And you know what do they say? 33% of your monthly income should go towards rent. That's a huge number. By the way, it should not be near that, but still it was doable."
[1:06:22]
"So, I did it. That moved me on to a small island in a much nicer apartment that looking back was a terrible apartment, but still was great for me because it wasn't the hood anymore and it was much nicer and I didn't have a roommate."
[1:06:38]
"This specific place I lived in for about a year. Then I leveled up to the next place which was 2,300 a month. Same island, different building, much nicer."
[1:06:52]
"Like tremendously tremendously nice for only what 700ish more a month. Same thing, very doable. At that time, I'd gone from 5k a month in income to maybe like eight to like 9K a month in income."
[1:07:08]
"I stayed in this 2,300 a month place. I actually started my business in the 1650 a month place. But when I got to the 2,300 a month place, I started to split live between LA and Miami."
[1:07:22]
"So, when I got to LA, there were two girls, my ex-girlfriend and her roommate, that allowed me to move into their apartment. So, they were paying 3,300 a month. I split that a third. So now I'm paying an extra 1,100 a month on top of the 2,300 a month I was already paying."
[1:07:42]
"At the same time, I started to get office rents. So my business is now making a lot of income and I'm starting to pay like six grand a month here in Miami. This is back when six grand would get you a lot in comparison to nowadays."
[1:07:58]
"And then I had two offices I got in Beverly Hills. So I'm probably at this point spending about 20ish-thousand, maybe 30ish-thousand give or take the month on office rents. And then about let's call it 35 to four grand on personal rent."
[1:08:15]
"Then I got rid of those three offices, came back to Miami and I moved into the penthouse. This is when it felt like a steal. I had 6,500 a month for the penthouse at the time. Great deal, you know, and now I'm feeling great. This awesome environment for me."
[1:08:35]
"This is actually where I started the inner circle was in that penthouse for those who have been in here for a long time. And moral of the story, this was technically a savings even though I was leveling up the amount I was spending on personal rent."
[1:08:50]
"Then I moved into the beach house that went from 6,500 to 8K a month. And then I moved here. The beach house went from 8K to 8,500 a month. This place was only 10K a month. So it started off with only a $1,500 difference between the beach house and here. That was a steal."
[1:09:10]
"And then everybody decided to move here, you know, good and bad. My rent here went from 10K a month to 18K a month. That one hit me pretty hard even though it was very affordable for me."
[1:09:25]
"You know, there was still a feeling of, 'Wow, wow, that seems pretty steep.' You know, wow. And you know, I just then I reminded myself I was like, 'Oh, I make way more than that, you know.'"
[1:09:38]
"So, you know, I was like, 'Yeah, let's do it.' And, you know, we locked it in. And then this place went from 18K to 19K in the most recent renewal."
[1:09:48]
"Now, here's my point. That's just one example of conditioning your way to a point where things become very easy, very very doable to afford. And every time that I'm spending that money, I'm also in belief that I'm going to spend it and then it's going to come back no matter what."
[1:10:08]
"It's something that I'm going to spend regardless. Did the same thing with clothes. So, back to my story with Grant telling me to go and spend my whole check."
[1:10:18]
"I had gone to a store called Lacoste, the little Gator polo brand. And I remember a polo there was like 110 bucks. Some of them were like 150ish. Dude, man, the first time I bought those, like my hand like shaking giving them the money, you know, felt like so much."
[1:10:38]
"And it took maybe three or four times of buying a shirt from there to eventually, you know, just make it normal. And then eventually I'd start buying more at a time instead of just like one shirt at a time."
[1:10:52]
"And eventually I walked across the street there on Lincoln Road to this place called Y3, Japanese designer brand, and their hoodies were like $400. And that's when I knew I was like, 'This is the next level cuz it made me shake again, you know.'"
[1:11:08]
"So I gave him 400 bucks for a hoodie in cash, you know, hands shaking. And you know, then you look back at that particular time frame, that was a big reward system for myself. I'd made my whole closet from Lacoste to Y3."
[1:11:25]
"And the point is, believe it or not, as dumb as spending an exceptional amount of money on clothes is, that conditioned me to be able to do things like invest into a mastermind easily, you know, because I was already spending thousands of dollars in clothes."
[1:11:42]
"Why wouldn't I be able to spend thousands of dollars in a mastermind that could help my personal growth? Those kind of things made it easier to go to like nicer restaurants. Those kind of things made it easier for me to buy a first class ticket and not really give a sh*t about it."
[1:12:00]
"Those kind of things made it easy to like take Uber black everywhere instead of like a regular Uber. So find an area in your life that you can essentially leverage where you're spending money that kind of makes you shake at first."
[1:12:15]
"And then it's just so easy to afford after like the third or fourth time doing it and then you'll see it carry over into other areas of your life."
[1:12:28]
"It's like as an example going courtside in Miami Heat game, 2500 a ticket. It's like dude that's no money, guys. That's like once again, you buy a first class ticket from like here to LA, you spend about the same amount of money."
[1:12:42]
"You see how that can carry over then going courtside and you're like, 'Oh, that's cheap.' So, it's like find an area where you can condition that you kind of carry over into other things."
[1:12:55]
"You go to nice restaurants, two people, you're dropping like $1,000 on a meal. It's like between the two of you, you're going to get about $1,000 in that check. You bring a whole squad of four, it's like going to be about 2,000 bucks."
[1:13:08]
"You go out with your friends, stop being plebs and splitting the bill. Just pay for the thing because it'll condition you once again. It carries over."
[1:13:18]
"Find those opportunities to essentially carry over the logic of this money's going to come back and it's going to also help me spending this money to be able to spend more money on things that are actually going to make me more money come time."
[1:13:35]
"Number 17, getting richer is a you game. Just it's very important to always note these kind of things that we're talking about. These are foundational belief systems that I believe all help perpetuate me getting more money in my life."
[1:13:52]
"Getting richer is not just the actions. It's not just sending more video pitches. It's not just spending more on ads, you know, it's not just taking more sales calls. These kind of ways of thinking behind the scenes, you know, it's all between the ears."
[1:14:08]
"These things are what actually reinforce that path and help you get richer. It's a you game. If you ever find yourself demotivated, not making as much money as you want, what you got to do is you got to ask and analyze, what am I missing in my thinking that's holding me back or preventing me from getting richer?"
[1:14:28]
"You know, what's holding me back from making 5 million a month? What's holding me back from getting to 500K a month? It's always 100% of the time something that is faulty within you that is preventing you from taking that action."
[1:14:45]
"There's a big difference between Jeremy that was in the hood and Jeremy that stands in front of you today. I've done an exercise with some of you in here before. It's the rebuilding your identity exercise. I learned it from Tony Robbins."
[1:15:00]
"My first time doing it. I had a giant sticky note and I wrote Jeremy 2.0 on it. I drew a little character and then I just put a bunch of dashes and I wrote the characteristics that I needed to be in order to advance to the next level."
[1:15:18]
"I stand in front of you today as Jeremy 37.0. That's how many times I've done that exercise and how many times I've redefined how I need to be thinking, what type of characteristics I need to view myself having in order to advance to that next step."
[1:15:35]
"It's all between the ears. That's where it all starts. And then it carries out. Your beliefs reinforce the actions that you take in a day or that you don't take in a day. The actions come from the beliefs."
[1:15:52]
"Number 18. You control the zone and staying in it, it's just as easy to fall out and just settle along the way. I live by this quote. I am a cold stone baller focused on the kill. Nothing takes me out of my zone. I stay locked in."
[1:16:10]
"I wake up every day. It is me versus me. I am responsible for getting in the zone. I am responsible for falling out of the zone. I am responsible for settling. I am responsible for having the energy that I need to get to that next level."
[1:16:28]
"It's so simple to stay tapped in to the necessary thoughts that you need to perpetuate to take consistent daily actions towards your financial goals you have. It's just as simple to do a bunch of sh*t in the day instead."
[1:16:42]
"I am responsible for staying in the zone and the zone benefits me to stay in. I am responsible for it."
[1:16:52]
"You'll notice I have Sonos everywhere in the house, constantly playing music that just gets me going, you know, gives me chills. I allow my environment around me to help stimulate how I want to be viewing myself, what type of energy I want to carry."
[1:17:08]
"The people that I talk to. All of it reinforces my worldview that I have. All of it keeps me in the zone. If I fall out of the zone, I have things that correct me to get back into it."
[1:17:22]
"And this kind of rolls into number 19. I want to build a world outside of me that helps carry me when internally I don't feel all there."
[1:17:35]
"You know how hard it is to be sad driving around in a 458 to go pick up a smoothie? Do you know how like you feel a little broke? Just look at your watch and be like, 'Oh, yeah, that's right.' You know, it's a bunch of sapphires and diamonds right there. That's cool."
[1:17:52]
"It's like, do you know how hard it is to like, guys, I'm not going to disclose this on camera, so make sure you cut this part out. It's like, if I'm down, I just go into my safe and just grab a wad of cash, you know? Just smell it. Just count it, you know? Just sit there, feel it."
[1:18:10]
"You know how hard it is to feel broke, you know, when you're just sitting there with a wad of cash, and just you're just holding a loaf, you know what I mean?"
[1:18:22]
"It's like, do you know how hard it is once again? Like to walk past your front door and look at 400 grand in cars and be like, man, I'm a broky over here. You know, it's like, no, you reinforce your internal worldview with external things."
[1:18:38]
"You know how hard it is to talk to my friends? Like the people I regularly talk to, you guys included. You all reinforce the world views that we want to see in one another's individual lives in our brains. We all carry the responsibility with one another."
[1:18:55]
"So do your friends. So do the people you regularly let into your life to help carry you when you internally aren't carrying yourself."
[1:19:08]
"That external world should be able to just automatically correct you and get you back in alignment, if you will."
[1:19:18]
"All right, cool. You guys got questions on this?"
[1:19:25]
[Q&A discussion begins about maintaining standards vs. financial fluidity]
[1:19:55]
"Well, that comes with that point. You know, you got to have fluidity with your standards when necessary if your income drops. Otherwise, you're going to be in a position where you kind of financially f*ck yourself."
[1:20:10]
"So, it's good to carry the mentality of I want these things and this is my standard. That creates the dissatisfaction when you fall below that standard. It's going to help lift you back up to that standard."
[1:20:28]
"Like if I can't drive the van downtown, you know, and I got to drive one of my cars, it's like I'm not financially ruined from doing that, you know? It's like it's a little unsatisfying to have to drive, you know, instead of be driven. But it's like whatever, you know? If I got to do it, I got to do it."
[1:20:48]
"If I can't fly private to go somewhere because it's like $50,000 and I don't want to drop that and I have to fly first class, I'm gonna allow the dissatisfaction to kind of sit inside of me and marinate a bit and make me feel less of myself as a motivating factor."
[1:21:10]
[Q&A about investment strategy when buying material things]
[1:21:18]
"So, I'll give you an example. So, like I spent 60K on the down payment on the 458, I immediately spent 60K into investments. It's like if I go out and I buy a watch for like 50 grand, I'm going to go and spend 50 grand on some kind of investment at the same time."
[1:21:38]
"That's just a rule of thumb I have to be able to buy dumb sh*t."
[1:21:48]
[Q&A about minimum threshold for this rule]
[1:21:52]
"It could literally be any amount of money. It's more so whatever I view as dumb. It could be a hundred bucks. Like, if I literally - I bought that new Spider-Man game the other day on the PS5 and I don't remember how much it was, but yeah, in real time, literally, I went in. I just transfer that same amount of money in there."
[1:22:12]
"Whatever you view is dumb. It's just a rule of thumb you can have for the most expensive things and the least expensive things."
[1:22:25]
[Q&A about specific investment accounts]
[1:22:30]
"So what I do is I have Bank of America and Merrill for that and my Bank of America and Merrill accounts are like linked in one login. So I can transfer the money from my Bank of America account into my Merrill account and then I open the Merrill app and I'll invest it into typically an index fund for that particular money."
[1:22:52]
"And then sometimes just nowadays with the trading of the futures in the morning. I'll also put like bigger lump sums into my Ninja Trader account to be able to invest on my own without having to use like the prop firms and then I'll actively trade that money."
[1:23:15]
[Q&A about ego delusion vs. realistic goals]
[1:23:35]
"To reconcile that paradox, you reach out to your mentors and peers so that the people - give and ask for feedback. People around - they'll tell you if you're delusional or not."
[1:23:52]
"Like somebody in the group the other day asked me if they should buy a Mont Blanc watch for like three grand and they were like I don't know if it's going to appreciate or not. And I was like no it's not going to appreciate - it's a piece of sh*t watch. Like don't buy that watch."
[1:24:08]
"Buy yourself like a cheap Rolex instead. And that's like little feedback on something that he wanted to buy that it would be a terrible just total waste of money. And then if he did choose to buy that I also literally put that caveat. I was like, if you're going to buy something like that, make sure you put that same amount in some type of investment account."
[1:24:28]
"Same guy asked me a couple days later if he should buy an M5. It was like 1,600 a month. He's making like 80 grand a month, you know? I was like, 'F*ck yeah, you should buy an M5.'"
[1:24:48]
[Q&A about Dave Ramsey]
[1:24:52]
"Dave Ramsey is like a character I demonize with like poor person financial advice that doesn't benefit me. Like I don't view it as making any sense to invest in mutual funds in comparison to like self-directed investments that I can make myself and cutting out that particular fee."
[1:25:10]
"Like he says no to all debt. I think that is bullsh*t. I think it makes no sense at all to not leverage debts to be able to get richer and to make more money when appropriate."
[1:25:25]
"There's just like very specific things that he actively preaches that I just disagree with. I'm sure there's things that he talks about that are beneficial, but as a character, I just demonize listening to anything that he says."
[1:25:38]
"And he's just not somebody that I actively like to listen to because it's important to note he has things that I actively disagree with that I don't want to allow into my echo chamber. So therefore, I deny the existence of his character in my echo chamber at all."
[1:25:55]
"So although yes, he could have good things that he says, I want only good things being said in my echo chamber."
[1:26:10]
[Q&A about getting back into the zone]
[1:26:15]
"Okay, so the other day I woke up, I was a little groggy. First thing I did, I go in the Ferrari 458 and I drive like 100 miles an hour up and down the road. And instantly my adrenaline is like so peaked and I came back and I got out the car and I was like yes, you know, like I'm back."
[1:26:38]
"And then I come in the house and I start playing Rick Ross and I I'm not joking like this room normally is just clear. I stand right there in the center of the room. I turn up the speakers really loud and I just blast some Rick Ross and it's like super bassy, you know."
[1:26:55]
"And I just stand there with my eyes closed for like two songs, you know, and then I'm just I'm just feeling right back to where I want to be mentally, you know?"
[1:27:08]
"And you could do that however you want. But that's just an example of like snapping out of that quick. There's plenty more examples, but that's just one set."
[1:27:25]
"All right. Lunch is ready. Go ahead and go into the kitchen. Grab some food."
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$19,000/mês - Aluguel atual da propriedade
$100,000/mês - Aluguel aspiracional
$5,000 - Gasto em um dia para tratamento de enxaqueca
$100,000/mês por deal - Meta de receita por cliente (10 deals = $1M/mês)
$60,000 - Entrada do Ferrari 458
$2,500 - Preço de ingresso courtside Miami Heat
$400 - Preço de hoodie Y3 (marca japonesa)
$110-150 - Preço polos Lacoste
$950 - Primeiro aluguel em Miami (dividido)
$6,500 - Aluguel do penthouse
$18,000-19,000 - Progressão de aluguel atual
37 versões - "Jeremy 37.0" (número de reconstruções de identidade)
80K-100K/mês - Faixa onde pessoas ficam "desconectadas"
300K+/mês - Pessoas que permanecem engajadas
$42,000 - Renda média em Ohio (cidade natal)
50,000 - População da cidade natal
5% - Taxa de juros atual em contas poupança
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Princípio da Honestidade Brutal
Identificar verdadeiros motivadores materiais
Não fazer "virtue signaling"
Usar vulnerabilidades de saúde como motivadores
Exercício: plano de 3 páginas para emergências
Identidade do Rico
Tornar-se conscientemente a pessoa rica que você imagina
Assimilação automática de características
Autoconsciência para evitar características negativas
Princípio da Eficiência (Compound Life)
Trazer todos os serviços para casa (médico, quiroprático, barbeiro)
Economia de 1.5 anos de tempo de deslocamento por década
Não otimizar diversão fora da equação
Decisões Temporárias vs Permanentes
Evitar dívidas que criam equity negativo
Contratos mês-a-mês vs anuais
Manter fluidez para desfazer decisões
Mentalidade de Ganhar vs Poupar
Gastar cheque inteiro (lição de Grant Cardone)
Perguntar "como ganhar esse valor de uma vez" vs economizar
Acreditar que dinheiro gasto voltará
Regra de Investimento Espelho
Para cada compra "boba", investir valor igual
Funciona desde $100 até $60,000+
Usar Merrill Lynch + Bank of America integrados
Sistema de Condicionamento de Gastos
Progressão: $110 polo → $400 hoodie → $2,500 courtside
Cada nível "treina" para o próximo
Mãos tremendo na primeira vez = nível certo
Gestão de Altos e Baixos
Manter despesas fluidas com receita
Metáfora do macaco e a noz
Saber quando "soltar a noz"
Echo Chamber Intencional
Instagram: apenas yachts, carros, propriedades de luxo
Zero conteúdo político
4-5 aquisições de $100M+ por dia no feed
Fazer $200K parecer "barato"
Progressão de Moradia
$475 → $1,650 → $2,300 → $6,500 → $8,000 → $10,000 → $18,000 → $19,000
Cada mudança era "acessível" no momento
Condicionamento progressivo
Exercício Jeremy 2.0 → 37.0
Sticky note com características necessárias
Reconstrução completa de identidade 37 vezes
Definir quem você precisa ser para o próximo nível
Zona de Fluxo
Minoria do dia: tarefas que não quer fazer
Maioria do dia: fluir entre o que quer fazer
Música ambiente (Sonos por toda casa)
Ambiente Geográfico
Ohio ($42K/ano média) vs Miami
Ser peixe pequeno em oceano grande
Mudar de cidade quando sentir estagnação
Gatilhos Externos de Correção
Dirigir Ferrari 100mph quando acordar mal
Abrir cofre e contar dinheiro
Rick Ross alto por 2 músicas
Olhar carros de $400K na frente de casa
Padrões de Relacionamento
História "Samantha" como exemplo negativo
"Quem conhece maçã perfeita: quem comeu 1000 ou 3?"
Padrões altíssimos para pessoas com mais exposição
ESTRUTURA:
19 Princípios Fundamentais:
Honestidade Brutal sobre motivações
Ser o rico que você pensa que um rico é
Eficiência (Compound Life)
Decisões temporárias vs permanentes
Dívida: ferramenta vs escravidão
Ganhar vs Poupar
Altos e baixos - fluidez de despesas
Permanecer conectado (não ficar "too good for help")
Echo Chamber intencional
Saúde primeiro (homem saudável tem 1000 desejos)
Parceiro e círculo próximo
100% comprado na ideia (vs ego delusion)
Gratidão ativa (evitar depressão)
Estado de Flow
Ambiente que te alimenta
Gastar livremente (responsavelmente)
Jogo interno ("you game")
Controlar a zona
Mundo externo que te carrega
Faixas de Comportamento:
Abaixo $80K/mês: Engajados
$81K-299K/mês: "Desconectados" (booby trap)
$300K+/mês: Altamente engajados
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Progressão de Renda:
Início: $3,000/mês (salário Grant Cardone)
Evolução: $5,000 → $8,000-9,000/mês
Meta articulada: $100K/mês por deal x 10 deals = $1M/mês
Contexto: Já fazia clientes atingirem $1M/mês líquido
Progressão de Aluguel Pessoal:
$475/mês (dividido, hood)
$1,650/mês (sem roommate)
$2,300/mês (upgrade)
$3,300/mês ÷ 3 = $1,100/mês (LA compartilhado)
$6,500/mês (penthouse)
$8,000/mês (beach house)
$10,000/mês (propriedade atual inicial)
$18,000/mês (aumento)
$19,000/mês (atual)
Aluguel de Escritórios:
$6,000/mês Miami (época de valor alto)
2 escritórios Beverly Hills
Total: $20,000-30,000/mês em escritórios
Investimentos em Saúde:
$5,000/dia para tratamento de enxaqueca
$5,000/mês high performance coach (Mark Dama)
Médico domiciliar, quiroprático, etc.
Compras Condicionadoras:
Polos Lacoste: $110-150
Hoodie Y3: $400
Ferrari 458: $60K entrada
Relógios: $50K+ (múltiplos)
Courtside: $2,500/ingresso
Refeições finas: $1,000 para 2 pessoas, $2,000 para 4
ROI de Tempo:
1.5 anos economizados por década (compound life)
Eliminação de tempo de espera (médicos, etc.)
Contexto Comparativo:
Ohio média: $42,000/ano
Messi aluguel: $300,000-500,000/mês
Loom aquisição: $975 milhões
Múltiplas aquisições $100M+ visualizadas diariamente
DURAÇÃO: ~1h 27min
FORMATO: Masterclass presencial com Q&A
